content:
Member-only story

3 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

Once you start extracting atomic facts, you immediately create a new problem: the same fact gets extracted hundreds of times. In our Support Intelligence Engine, one common root cause might appear in 400 tickets. Extract naively and you get 400 near-identical fact records. Retrieve and they crowd out everything else — and worse, the 400 versions subtly disagree.
Most teams treat this as cleanup. The better framing: deduplication isn’t janitorial; it’s where you manufacture your most valuable relevance signal.
Detecting duplicates with similarity
Each fact has an embedding, so you can measure how alike two facts are using cosine similarity — a score from 0 (unrelated) to 1 (identical meaning). The consolidation logic uses thresholds:

• Above ~0.92 → merge. These two facts mean the same thing; collapse them into one.

• A middle band (~0.85 to ~0.97) with genuinely different content → flag as a conflict rather than merging. This catches the case where two sources say almost the same thing but disagree on a detail (“affects v4.1 and earlier” vs “affects v4.2 and earlier”). You don’t want to silently pick one; you want a human to reconcile it.

• Below the band → keep separate.
